---
title: "R Code"
author: "John D Williams"
date: "2026-03-29"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## My Basic R Code

```{r}
#Total paper
#Preliminaries
library(dplyr)
library(tidyverse)
```

## Preliminary
```{r}
setwd("C:/Users/Owner/Documents/BallState/DSCI-609 SocSci/Paper")
gss_data <- read.csv("gss_data.csv")

# Keep columns where the count of non-NA values is greater than 0
gss_data1 <- gss_data[, colSums(is.na(gss_data)) < nrow(gss_data)]

gss_data1$sex <- as_factor(gss_data1$sex)

# Cleaning missing values before analysis [cite: 69]
gss_data1$happy[gss_data1$happy > 3] <- NA
gss_data1$goodlife[gss_data1$goodlife > 5] <- NA
gss_data1$satfin[gss_data1$satfin > 6000] <- NA
gss_data1$sex[gss_data1$sex > 2] <- NA
gss_data1$degree[gss_data1$degree > 4] <- NA
gss_data1$realinc[gss_data1$realinc > 100000] <- NA
gss_data1$age[gss_data1$age > 90] <- NA
gss_data1$polviews[gss_data1$polviews > 7] <- NA

# Narrow the scope to the 3–5 key variables suggested in the approach [cite: 36, 37]
gss_data2 <- gss_data1[, c("happy", "goodlife", "satfin", "degree", "realinc", "age", "sex", "polviews")]
```

## Batch 1
```{r}
#Batch1
library(tidyverse)
library(car) # For Levene's Test
library(ggplot2)

# 1. Precise Cleaning for GSS codes
# We must ensure 'sex' is a factor and 'goodlife' is numeric and filtered
gss_clean <- gss_data2 %>%
  mutate(
    # Convert goodlife to numeric and handle GSS missing codes (0, 8, 9) [cite: 68-72]
    goodlife_num = as.numeric(as.character(goodlife)),
    goodlife_num = ifelse(goodlife_num > 5, NA, goodlife_num),
    
    # Force sex to be a Factor (this fixes the Levene's Test error) 
    sex_factor = as.factor(sex)
  ) %>%
  # Filter to keep ONLY Male and Female and remove any remaining NAs
  filter(sex_factor %in% c("male", "female"), !is.na(goodlife_num)) %>%
  # Drop unused levels like 'skipped on web' or 'iap'
  mutate(sex_factor = fct_drop(sex_factor))

# 2. Verify the Descriptive Statistics
# You should see actual numbers for Mean and SD now
gss_clean %>%
  group_by(sex_factor) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean = mean(goodlife_num),
    SD = sd(goodlife_num),
    N = n()
  )

# 3. Homogeneity of Variance (Levene's Test)
# This will now run because sex_factor is an explicit factor 
lt <- leveneTest(goodlife_num ~ sex_factor, data = gss_clean)
print(lt)

# 4. Independent Samples t-test
# We compare the means of these two groups [cite: 91, 99]
t_test_results <- t.test(goodlife_num ~ sex_factor, data = gss_clean, var.equal = TRUE)

print(t_test_results)

# 5. Prepare the plotting data (Calculating Standard Error)
batch1_plot_data <- gss_clean %>%
  group_by(sex_factor) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean = mean(goodlife_num, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD = sd(goodlife_num, na.rm = TRUE),
    N = n(),
    SE = SD / sqrt(N)  # Formula for Standard Error
  )

# 6. Create the Bar Chart
ggplot(batch1_plot_data, aes(x = sex_factor, y = Mean, fill = sex_factor)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.6, color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Mean - SE, ymax = Mean + SE), width = 0.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Economic Optimism by Gender",
    subtitle = "Error bars represent +/- 1 Standard Error",
    x = "Gender",
    y = "Mean Optimism Score (Lower = More Optimistic)",
    caption = "Source: General Social Survey (GSS)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("female" = "#f8766d", "male" = "#00bfc4")) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") # Hide legend as X-axis labels are clear
```

## Batch 2
```{r}
#Batch2
# --- Rebuild gss_clean from gss_data2 with correct sex coding ---
gss_clean <- gss_data2 %>%
  mutate(
    goodlife_num = as.numeric(as.character(goodlife)),
    goodlife_num = ifelse(goodlife_num > 5, NA, goodlife_num),
    sex_factor = factor(tolower(trimws(as.character(sex))),
                        levels = c("male", "female"))
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(goodlife_num), !is.na(sex_factor)) %>%
  mutate(sex_factor = fct_drop(sex_factor))

# 1. Prepare the Data
gss_batch2 <- gss_clean %>%
  mutate(
    degree_factor = as.factor(degree),
    degree_factor = fct_recode(degree_factor,
                               "LT High School" = "0",
                               "High School" = "1",
                               "Junior College" = "2",
                               "Bachelor" = "3",
                               "Graduate" = "4"
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(degree_factor))

# Generate the Table Values for Batch 2
ipv <- gss_batch2 %>%
  group_by(degree_factor) %>%
  summarise(
    Mean_Optimism = mean(goodlife_num, na.rm = TRUE),
    Std_Dev = sd(goodlife_num, na.rm = TRUE),
    N = n()
  )
print(ipv)

# 2. Run the Two-Way ANOVA
anova_results <- aov(goodlife_num ~ sex_factor * degree_factor, data = gss_batch2)

# 3. View the ANOVA Table
summary(anova_results)

# 4. Interaction Plot
interaction.plot(gss_batch2$degree_factor, gss_batch2$sex_factor, gss_batch2$goodlife_num,
                 xlab = "Highest Degree Attained",
                 ylab = "Mean GOODLIFE Score",
                 main = "Interaction of Gender and Education on Optimism",
                 col = c("blue", "red"), lty = 1, lwd = 2,
                 ylim = c(2.0, 2.9))

```

## Preliminary Part 2
```{r}
library(WDI)
# Pull with a more stable GDP code:
# NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.CD is GDP per capita, PPP (current international $)
world_data <- WDI(
  country = "all",
  indicator = c(
    Life_Expectancy = "SP.DYN.LE00.IN",
    GDP_per_capita  = "NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.CD", 
    Health_Spend    = "SH.XPD.CHEX.GD.ZS",
    Education_Rate  = "SE.SEC.ENRR"
  ),
  start = 2005,
  end = 2022,
  extra = FALSE
)

#Added to get regions
#Access the built-in country metadata
country_info <- as.data.frame(WDI_data$country)

# Select only the columns we need: ISO code and Region
region_map <- country_info[, c("iso2c", "region")]

# Merge it into your world_data
world_data_with_regions <- merge(world_data, region_map, by = "iso2c", all.x = TRUE)

# Filter for rows that are actual countries (removes aggregate regions like 'Arab World')
world_data1 <- world_data_with_regions[world_data_with_regions$region != "Aggregates", ]

# Filter for rows that are actual countries (removes aggregate regions like 'Arab World')
world_data2 <- world_data1[world_data1$region != "Aggregates", ]

# Define the columns you want to get rid of
unwanted_cols <- c("iso2c")

world_data3 <- world_data2 %>%
  # Drop only the columns that actually exist in the data
  dplyr::select(-one_of(intersect(names(.), unwanted_cols))) %>%
  
  # Rename country to country_name
  rename(country_name = country)
```

## Batch 3
```{r}
#Batch3
# --- Batch 3: Multiple Regression ---
# Dataset: WDI
# DV: Life_Expectancy
# IVs: GDP_per_capita, Health_Spend, Education_Rate
library(ggfortify)

# 1. Run the Regression Model
# Using log() for GDP as recommended in the Paper Approach [cite: 170]
regression_model <- lm(Life_Expectancy ~ log(GDP_per_capita) + Health_Spend + Education_Rate, 
                       data = world_data3)

# 2. View the Results
summary(regression_model)
# Create both plots side-by-side
autoplot(regression_model, which = 1:2, ncol = 2) + 
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Diagnostic Plots for Global Life Expectancy Model",
       caption = "Batch 3: WDI Regression Analysis")
```

## Batch 4
```{r}
#Batch4
library(mediation)
library(DiagrammeR)

# 1. Clean and Prepare GSS Data
gss_med <- gss_data2 %>%
  mutate(
    # Reverse code happy: Higher = Happier (3 = Very Happy, 1 = Not Too Happy)
    happy_rev = case_when(
      happy == 1 ~ 3,
      happy == 2 ~ 2,
      happy == 3 ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    ),
    # Ensure income and degree are numeric
    income_num = as.numeric(as.character(realinc)),
    edu_num = as.numeric(as.character(degree))
  ) %>%
  drop_na(happy_rev, income_num, edu_num)

# 2. Path A: Education -> Income
fit.mediator <- lm(income_num ~ edu_num, data = gss_med)

# 3. Path B & C': Education + Income -> Happiness
fit.dv <- lm(happy_rev ~ edu_num + income_num, data = gss_med)

# 4. Run the Mediation Analysis
med_results <- mediate(fit.mediator, fit.dv, treat = "edu_num", mediator = "income_num", boot = TRUE, sims = 100)

# 5. Summary
summary(med_results)

# Path a (Education -> Income)
model_a <- lm(scale(income_num) ~ scale(edu_num), data = gss_med)
beta_a <- round(coef(model_a)[2], 3)

# Path b & c' (Income and Education -> Happiness)
model_bc <- lm(scale(happy_rev) ~ scale(edu_num) + scale(income_num), data = gss_med)
beta_c_prime <- round(coef(model_bc)[2], 3) # Direct effect
beta_b <- round(coef(model_bc)[3], 3)       # Mediator effect

# Print them to use in the diagram
cat("Path a:", beta_a, "\nPath b:", beta_b, "\nPath c':", beta_c_prime)

library(DiagrammeR)

grViz("
  digraph mediation {
    # General Graph Settings
    graph [layout = dot, rankdir = LR]
    node [shape = box, fontname = Helvetica, penwidth = 2]
    
    # Define the Nodes (X, M, Y)
    X [label = 'Education\n(X)']
    M [label = 'Real Income\n(M)']
    Y [label = 'Happiness\n(Y)']

    # Define the Paths (Arrows)
    # Using your actual Batch 4 results:
    X -> M [label = 'Path a (Significant)']
    M -> Y [label = 'Path b (Significant)']
    
    # Path c-prime is the 'ADE' from your PDF (0.00018, p = 0.94)
    # We use a dashed line because it is NOT significant
    X -> Y [label = 'Direct (c-prime) = 0.00 (ns)', style = dashed]
    
    # Add the Total Effect and Indirect Effect as a note
    edge [fontname = Helvetica, fontsize = 10]
    
    # Optional: Label the indirect effect (ACME)
    X -> M -> Y [color = blue, label = 'Indirect (ACME) = 0.034***']
  }
")
```

## Batch 5
```{r}
#Batch5
library(tidyverse)
library(lme4)
library(performance)
library(sjPlot)
library(ggplot2)

setwd("C:/Users/Owner/Documents/BallState/DSCI-609 SocSci/Paper")

#Load the raw data
oecd_raw <- read.csv("oced_bli.csv")

#Create the Regional Mapping
# We must ensure North America and Sub-Saharan Africa are distinct groups
region_map <- data.frame(
  country_name = c(
    "Australia", "Austria", "Belgium", "Brazil", "Canada", "Chile", "Colombia", 
    "Costa Rica", "Czechia", "Denmark", "Estonia", "Finland", "France", "Germany", 
    "Greece", "Hungary", "Iceland", "Ireland", "Israel", "Italy", "Japan", "Korea", 
    "Latvia", "Lithuania", "Luxembourg", "Mexico", "Netherlands", "New Zealand", 
    "Norway", "Poland", "Portugal", "Russia", "Slovak Republic", "Slovenia", 
    "South Africa", "Spain", "Sweden", "Switzerland", "Türkiye", "United Kingdom", 
    "United States"
  ),
  region = c(
    "East Asia & Pacific", "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "Latin America & Caribbean", "North America", "Latin America & Caribbean", 
    "Latin America & Caribbean", "Latin America & Caribbean", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "Middle East & North Africa", "Europe & Central Asia", "East Asia & Pacific", 
    "East Asia & Pacific", "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "Europe & Central Asia", "Latin America & Caribbean", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "East Asia & Pacific", "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "Europe & Central Asia", "Sub-Saharan Africa", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", "Europe & Central Asia", 
    "Europe & Central Asia", "North America"
  )
)

#Clean and Pivot the Data
batch_5_data <- oecd_raw %>%
  dplyr::filter(
    Indicator %in% c(
      "Life satisfaction", 
      "Quality of support network", 
      "Employment rate", 
      "Household net adjusted disposable income", 
      "Housing expenditure"
    ),
    Inequality == "Total"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(Country, Indicator, OBS_VALUE) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = Indicator, values_from = OBS_VALUE) %>%
  dplyr::rename(
    country_name = Country,
    life_satisfaction = `Life satisfaction`,
    social_support = `Quality of support network`,
    employment_rate = `Employment rate`,
    GDP_per_capita = `Household net adjusted disposable income`,
    housing_affordability = `Housing expenditure`
  ) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(region_map, by = "country_name") %>%
  # Crucial: This is where your previous code stopped/broke.
  # We drop rows with missing values to ensure the Factor Analysis has data to work with.
  drop_na(life_satisfaction, social_support, employment_rate, GDP_per_capita, housing_affordability, region)

#Fit a Null Multilevel Model
mlm_null <- lmer(life_satisfaction ~ 1 + (1 | region), data = batch_5_data)
cat("Null Model ICC (Regional variance focus):\n")
print(performance::icc(mlm_null))

#Fit the final requested model
mlm_final <- lmer(life_satisfaction ~ social_support + (1 | region), data = batch_5_data)
summary(mlm_final)

# Create the Caterpillar Plot for the final model
plot_model(mlm_final, type = "re", show.values = TRUE, value.offset = .3) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Caterpillar Plot: Regional Variation in Life Satisfaction",
    subtitle = "Random Intercepts by World Region (Batch 5)",
    y = "Adjustment to Global Intercept",
    x = "Region"
  ) +
  # Adds a horizontal line at 0 (the global average)
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red")
```

## Batch 6
```{r}
#Batch6
library(tidyverse)
library(psych)

#Select the indicators for the Quality of Life Factor
# Now we select the numeric columns from our clean batch_5_data
efa_data <- batch_5_data %>%
  dplyr::select(
    GDP_per_capita, 
    housing_affordability, 
    employment_rate, 
    social_support, 
    life_satisfaction
  )

#Suitability tests
print(KMO(cor(efa_data)))
print(bartlett.test(efa_data))

# 1. Run the Parallel Analysis and Scree Plot
# n.iter = 100 runs a simulation to show where 'random' factors would fall
parallel_results <- fa.parallel(
  efa_data, 
  fm = "minres", # Minimum Residual factor method
  fa = "fa",     # Looking specifically for Factors
  n.iter = 100, 
  main = "Scree Plot: Identifying the 'Quality of Life' Factor"
)

# 2. Add a horizontal line at Eigenvalue = 1 (Kaiser Criterion)
abline(h = 1, col = "red", lty = 2)
```
